California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
William R. McKown 
Acting city Administrator 
City of Carson 
P.O. Box 6234 
Carson, CA 
90749 
Dear Mr. McKown: 
August 19, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request For Advice 
Our File No. A-88-276 
You have requested advice about application of section 
89001 of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")Y, as amended by 
recently enacted Proposition 73, to the city of Carson monthly 
report. 
QUESTIONS 
You asked the following questions about the January and 
June 1988 copies of the City of Carson monthly report entitled 
liThe Carson Report. II 
1. 
May the city publish portrait photographs of its 
elected officials in the monthly report? 
2. 
May the city publish group photographs of the city's 
elected officials in the report, if the officials are not 
identified by name? 
3. 
May the city publish a feature article introducing a 
newly appointed councilmember? 
Y 
Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code 
of Regulations section 18000, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code 
of Regulations. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


William R. McKown 
August 19, 1988 
Page -2-
4. 
Is there a difference between a report being mailed at 
public expense and being delivered at public expense? 
CONCLUSIONS 
The city may not publish photographs of or otherwise refer 
to elected officers in the city's monthly report or in any 
other mass mailing. 
This prohibition includes publishing group 
photographs in which elected city officers are not identified 
by name. 
Furthermore, for purposes of the Act a person 
appointed to a vacant elective office is an elected officer. 
Therefore, the city may not publish a feature article about a 
newly appointed councilmember. 
Nevertheless, the city may list the names of elected 
officers in a standard roster-type listing, as long as names of 
all elected officers are in the same type size and typeface. 
The Commission also has concluded that a public agency may 
not send a mass mailing in any way that results in expenditure 
of public moneys. 
Delivering a document is a form of sending a 
document. 
Therefore, the city may not spend public moneys to 
deliver mass mailings that contain the name, signature, or 
photograph of an elected officer. 
FACTS 
The city of Carson publishes a monthly report entitled "The 
Carson Report." 
On the address page of the January 1988 
report, the city published captioned portrait photographs of 
the city's seven elected officers: 
five councilmembers, the 
city clerk and city treasurer. 
The June 1988 report had several references to elected city 
officers. 
That issue's front-page feature article introduced a 
newly appointed member of the city council; the article 
included a large portrait photograph of the official. 
Inside 
the report, three different group photographs showed several 
councilmembers, who were identified by name in the captions. 
Finally, the address page had captioned portrait photographs of 
the city's seven elected officers. 
ANALYSIS 
California electors enacted proposition 73 in June 1988. 
Proposition 73 includes amendments to Sections 89001 and 


William R. McKown 
August 19, 1988 
Page -3-
82041.5 of the Act. 
These amendments took effect on June 8, 
1988. 
section 89001 now reads: 
89001. 
No newsletter or other mass mailing shall 
be sent at public expense. 
section 82041.5 now reads: 
82041.5. 
"Mass mailing" means two hundred or 
more substantially similar pieces of mail, but does 
not include a form letter or other mail which is sent 
in response to an unsolicited request, letter or other 
inquiry. 
These two sections could be construed to ban all 
mailings at public expense of 200 or more substantially 
similar documents. 
On July 26, 1988, however, the 
Commission adopted an emergency regulation and approved 
interim advice that allows public agencies to send certain 
mass mailings at public expense. 
(Regulation 18901; Raye 
Advice Letters, No. A-88-220, copies enclosed.) 
In the 
following paragraphs we apply emergency Regulation 18901 
and the interim advice letters to the monthly reports 
published by the city of Carson. 
Prohibition Against Spending Public Moneys To Promote Elected 
Officials 
After considering ballot arguments in favor of Proposition 
73, statements by the proponents of the proposition, and the 
language of amended section 89001, the Commission concluded 
that the intent of section 89001 is to prohibit the use of 
public moneys for the purpose of sending out mass mailings 
which assist elected officials in the quest for public office. 
(Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, June 16, 1988.). 
Therefore, 
any reference to an elected officer is prohibited in a public 
agency newsletter sent to 200 or more persons in a calendar 
month, except in the standard letterhead or in a roster-type 
listing in which each elected officer's name appears in the 
same type size and typeface as the other officers. 
(See 
emergency Regulation 18901; Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, 
July 1, 1988.) 
Consequently, the city may not publish portrait or group 
photographs of elected officers in the monthly report, even if 
the captions do not identify the officers by name. 
Also, 
Section 82020 defines an elected officer to include "a person 
who is appointed to fill a vacant elective office .... " 
Thus, a 


William R. McKown 
August 19, 1988 
Page -4-
newly appointed councilmember is an elected officer for 
purposes of the Act. 
Therefore, the city cannot publish and 
send a feature article about the newly appointed councilmember 
or use a photograph of the officer. 
(Also see Gonzalves Advice 
Letter, No. A-88-021, copy enclosed.) 
The city however, may 
list its elected officers in the standard letterhead or in a 
roster-type listing that uses the same type size and typeface 
for each officer. 
Sending A Mass Mailing 
For the meantime, the Commission has concluded that section 
89001 prohibits a public agency from spending public moneys to 
distribute mass mailings that contain the names or photographs 
of elected officers. 
(Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, July 
1, 1988, at page 5.) 
The terms "sent" and "mailing" include 
delivering documents. (Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, July 
I, 1988, at page 6, footnote 4.) 
Therefore, the city may not 
use public moneys to pay for any type of delivery service to 
distribute a mass mailing containing the name or photograph of 
an elected officer. 
I hope this letter provides satisfactory answers to your 
advice request. 
Please call me at (916) 322-5901 if you have 
any questions about this letter. 
DMG:MA:aa 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
argarita Altamirano 
Counsel, Legal Division

CITY 0

July 12, 1988 
Fair Political Practice Commission 
428 J Street, Suite 800 
Sacramento, California 95814 
Attention: Legal Division 
Please find attached two copies of Monthly Reports for the City of Carson, 
California. Each copy is marked in yellow as items to be deleted per our 
understanding of your staff's interpretation of newly enacted Proposition 
73. I would appreciate specific comments on each report as to the following: 
1. 
Does the yellow marked deletions bring the Report within 
the FPPC Guidelines • 
2. 
Are group pictures such as those in Report # 2 dated June 
1988 acceptable if Council names are deleted? 
3. 
Can a one time feature article introducing a newly appointed 
Councilmember be used as a Public Information piece. 
4. 
Is there a FPPC distinction made between mailed at public 
expense and delivered at public expense. 
I would appreciate your immediate response so the City can modify our 
Monthly Report to comply with the FPPC rules and still provide informative 
reports to the City of Carson. 
Sincerely, 
w~g/;nC~ 
William R. McKown 
Acting City Administrator

Attachments

2.

1988 acceptable if Council names are deleted? 
3. 
Can a one time feature article introducing a newly appointed 
Council member be used as a Public Information piece. 
4. 
Is there a FPPC distinction made between mailed at public 
expense and delivered at public expense. 
I would appreciate your immediate response so the City can modify our 
Monthly Report to comply with the FPPC rules and still provide informative 
reports to the City of Carson. 
Sincerely,

JANUARY 1988 
Carson City Government 
Reorganization -1988 
In an effort to provide the residents 
and taxpayers a more efficient and 
responsive City Government, the 
Carson City Council approved a total 
reorganization plan for city staff and 
services on December 7,1987. 
The proposed changes will begin in 
January, 1988, and will take approx-
imately six to eight months to become 
totally operational. 
The City Council in this consolidated 
effort desires to structure a City Gov-
ernment where citizen 
and 
efficient responses go hand-in-hand. 
According to Councilmembers the 
reorganization will assist each resident 
in knowing who to contact if and when 
they feel there is a need for public ser-
vice. Along with the Reorganization 
Chart, shown on the 2nd page of this 
Carson Report, are the appropriate 
names and telephone numbers of 
Administrators who supervise the 
activities. 
This information will assist Carson 
citizens who need public services or 
information. Councilmembers felt that 
it is important to give the Government 
a general tune-up to make it more 
responsive to your present day needs. 
They hope you will keep this report 
available to assist you in- receiving 
responsive services from Carson 
City Hall. 
City Council 
Changes 
Meeting Day 
And Time 
Beginning January 5, 1988, City 
Council meetings will be held on the 
first and third Tuesdays of every 
month. Don't be late because the time 
of the council meetings has also 
changed and will be starting a half an 
hour earlier, moving the meeting time 
from 6:30 P.M. to 6:00 P.M., with the 
Redevelopment Agency 
fol-
lowing at 7:30 P.M.

Remodeling Of 
City Hall's North 
Wing Complete 
Remodeling to fire damaged City Hall is 
completed. 
The 
Carson City Council 
has 
announced that work has been com-
pleted on Carson City Hall's north 
wing, which was heavily damaged, as a 
result of a fire early in 1987. The 
reconstruction of the north wing was 
completed in eight months, and city 
officials say that repairs will begin on 
the rest of the damaged City Hall 
within 60 days. 
North wing, which was remodeled 
to resemble its pre-fire structure, was 
open to bids for reconstruction. Dam-
age estimates on the section, which 
began at $500,000, ran well over 
$1,000,000. Additionally, the rest of 
City Hall must be repainted and dam-
aged ceiling tiles replaced. 
The fire, which caused over $1 mil-
lion in total damage, was set by an 
arsonist who broke in through a win-
dow in the north 
Community 
Development Department. The blaze 
which caused not only the extensive 
damage also 
operations 
for one week . 


The Carson Report -January 1988 
City Crowns 
New Miss Carson 
Camille Tucker, a UCLA student. receives her crown after winning the recent Miss Carson pageant 
competItIOn, Shown wIth her III the pIcture are (from left to right) Miss California Simone Stephens; 
Tucker:, the outgoing Miss Carson. Bridget Anita Jones; and Cynthia Simpson. first runner-up, Not 
shown IS Conme Howard. second runner-up and Kally Lewis. Miss Congeniality. 
The new Miss Carson Camille 
Tucker, a UCLA student, received a 
$1,000 scholarship and will be making 
many public appearances as requested 
by the city or the chamber of com-
merce. She has also earned the right to 
compete for the Miss California title in 
San Diego on June 13,1988. 
The queen's court includes 1st run-
ner up Cynthia Symphson, 
who 
received a $500 scholarship, and 2nd 
runner up Connie Howard, 
who 
received a $300 scholarship. In addi-
tion to receiving scholarships, the 
Queen and her court also received 
memberships to the 1MB Academy 
Martial Arts and Fitness Center. Kally 
Lewis, who was chosen by the partici-
pants in the pageant to be Miss Conge-
niality, received a trophy and a $200 
scholarship. 
The Miss Carson Pageant, pre-
sented by the Carson Chamber of 
Commerce and the City of Carson, is 
the preliminary to the Miss California! 
Miss America Pageant (a scholarship 
pageant). This year's pageant was held 
at the Carson Community Center on 
November 22, 1987, when twelve 
young ladies competed for the title of 
Miss Carson. 
Contestants were judged in four 
areas of competition, which included 
an interview (7)-'2 minutes in duration), 
swim suit, and evening gown and 
talent competition. 
The Miss Carson Pageant has been 
in existence since the incorporation of 
the City in 1968, but was not fran-
chised with the Miss CalifornialMiss 
America Pageant until July 1985. 
The Pageant's purpose is to distrib-
ute money to young ladies wishing to 
further their education. 
This 
is 
achieved through the awarding of 
scholarships to not only the new queen 
and her court but to all of the partici-
pants for their involvement in the pag-
eant. This year each girl received a 
$100 scholarship for her participation 
in the pageant. 
Plaques 
of 
appreciation 
were 
awarded to corporate sponsors for 
their participation in the pageant. 
Among those present were John Moyer 
of the Shell Oil Company, Howard 
Duffy of the Carson #438 Moose 
Lodge, and Richard S. Bustello of 1MB 
Academy Martial Arts and Fitness 
Center. Also present was the Presi-
dent of the Miss California Pageant, 
Robert Arnhym. 
Free Tax 
Program For 
Elderly And 
Disabled 
The Senior Social Services Depart-
ment is offering assistance to Seniors 
and the disabled in filling out state and 
federal tax returns. 
The tax counseling program is co-
sponsored by the City of Carson, the 
Internal Revenue Service, and the 
Franchise Tax Board. Last year, the 
program helped an estimated 400 
people in filling out state and federal 
tax returns. 
The program is staffed by trained 
volunteers and is offered free of 
charge. 
For futher information about the 
program please contact the Carson 
Senior Social Services at 801 E. 
Carson Street, or call 835-0212, ext. 
203 or 208. 
Council Recap 
November 30, 1987 
- Council approved a resolution adopt-
ing the City Budget and making 
appropriations for the 1987-88 fiscal 
year. 
December 1, 1987 
- Voted to support efforts to de-annex 
the northern most section of Carson 
from the Compton School District 
and annex it into the Los Angeles 
Unified School District. 
- Voted to support the concept of a 
satellite municipal court in Carson 
and directed staff to study the mat-
ter and prepare a report for Council. 
December 7, 1987 
• Carson Redevelopment Agency 
voted to award a 6.2 million dollar 
contract for construction of Veterans 
Park. Project to begin in near 
future. 
• Passed ordinance to establish 
zoning regulations for cargo con-
tainer storage facilities. 


The Carson Report • Jan uary 1988 
The City Of Carson's Year In Review 
1987 was an exciting and productive 
year for the City of Carson. Besides 
the individual accomplishments of so 
many city residents. City government 
produced a variety of services for the 
good of the community. 
PARKS AND RECREATION had its 
usual busy year in 1987. Special events 
included the Annual Anniversary 
Celebration, 
Cultural 
Fair, 
Miss 
Carson Pageant, the Fine Arts Day 
Camp, The Carson Symphony, Los 
Caballeros Youth Band, Young Life 
Singers, Children's Playbox Theatre, 
the Tiny Tot programs (including the 
1987 Mini-Olympics), and the many, 
many fine excursions to such places as 
Las Vegas, Tijuana and San Diego. 
SENIORS had a wide range of pro-
grams from which to choose, such as 
the Dial-A-Taxi program, the Friendly 
Visitor program, and one of 1987's 
highlights, Carson's hosting of the Cal-
ifornia Senior Legislature Candidates 
Forum and the election of Carson's first 
Senior Assemblyman. 
Other SPECIAL EVENTS in Car-
son included the 4th of july Spectacu-
lar, Boxing Dinner Shows, Mardi Gras 
Celebration, Annual Country Western 
Fair, the Jazz Festival, Cinco de Mayo, 
Mexican Independence Day, Tribute to 
Dr. Martin Luther King, Winter Holi-
day Festival, Asian Pacific Islander 
Day, Culture in Black Festival, Hal-
loween carnivals at two park sites and 
the Samoan Athletes in ActionIYouth 
Sports Clinic. 
Gospel singers such as "The Nicholas Family" 
captioned will appear at Carson's 4th annual 
Martin Luther King Jr. TrIbute Jan. 16 from 
noon to 5 p.m. at Avalon Park. 
Even though the stock market this 
year was highly volatile, reserves 
maintained by the City and Redevelop-
ment Agency were invested wisely, 
resulting in substantial returns. 
Reserves increased by nearly $15 
million and an additional $4 million 
were transferred to the 
Fund 
to pay for needed City services. 
THE COMMUNITY DEVELOP-
MENT DEPARTMENT, made up of 
three divisions, redevelopment, plan-
ning and building and safety respon-
sibilities also reports accomplishments 
for last year. 
The department continued careful 
in-depth zoning studies related to 
industrial activities; sponsored the 
Arbor Day program, planting trees in 
City parks; sponsored an anti-litter 
program and promoted the annual Car-
son Beautiful program to recognize the 
homes and buildings which improve 
the beauty of the city. 
REDEVELOPMENT is taking a 
more and more active role in the City. 
The new Carson Ibis Hotel and office 
complex broke ground in September of 
1987. The project will consist of a 
seven story, 225-room hotel and 
120,000 square foot office building 
adjacent to the Carson Community 
Building. The complex will result in 
additional revenues to the City and cre-
ate over 200 new jobs. 
THE VETERANS PARK site is in 
the final stages of cleanup. After the 
City discovered hazardous waste in the 
soil, they moved immediately to exca-
vate and clean the soil. On December 
7, 1987, the Council awarded a $6.2 
million contract for construction of the 
park facility. 
MAJOR CONSTRUCTION PROJ-
ECTS on City streets were completed 
during 1987, including Gardena Blvd., 
Wilmington. Main Street at 223rd 
Street. and curb. gutter and sidewalk 
improvements at 219th and Moneta. 
Also. Sepulveda Boulevard was 
rebuilt between the Harbor Freeway 
and Main Street. the largest public 
works project taken on by the City last 
year. 
Sepulveda Blvd. reconstruction - limn Harbor 
Freeway to Main St. - $835,000 project. 
Project included street resllJfacing, Imldscap-
ing of meridians, Ill'W street lighting and 
sidewalks. 
THE SERVICE REQUEST DESK, 
which is the City's central locale for 
people with questions about services, 
handled 
over 
1,850 
calls 
from 
residents. 
THE 
EMERGENCY 
SERVICES 
SECTION was the lead departments in 
preparing the new Multi-Hazard Func-
tional Plan for this area of Los Angeles 
County, which is made up of 28 cities. 
After such an eventful year in 1987, 
one might expect a year of rest. But 
1988 promises to be just as exciting a 
year for Carson!

SON 
PO 
OF CARSON, CALIFORNIA

June 1988 
Carson's New-est Council1De1Dber 
John H. Anderson: 
X 1/ 
A True Civic Servant 
~ 
Carson's newest Councilmember, 
John Anderson, is no stranger to 
serving the communtiy: he's been 
volunteering his athletic, musical 
and social talents to Carson Seniors 
for over 19 years. 
A tennis pro, the 70-year old 
Councilman gives lessons to Sen-
iors through the recreation depart-
ment and he is said to give private 
lessons "to any young person who 
asks" at Bixby Knolls, his local 
court. 
Anderson is also an accomplish-
ed pianist. Of course, he has also 
donated this special talent to the 
Seniors of Carson, often delivering 
performances to local Senior groups. 
His administrative experience 
with the City of Carson includes 
planning and supervising special 
events, organizing numerous Senior 
Citizen recreation programs and 
serving on the Parks and Recrea-
tion Commission. As recognition 
for his many years of public service, 
the Councilman was also nominat-
ed for the John Anson Ford Award 
for outstanding volunteer work 
with senior citizens. 
Councilman Anderson retired 
from his 15-year career as a real 
estate manager at Trust Properties 
in 1981. He previously worked for 
Security Pacific Bank and the fed-
eral government, but there was a 
time when Anderson had to make a 
difficult decision between playing 
football with the New York Black 
Bombers and using his college de-
gree towards a conventional job. 
Anderson chose the latter. 
"At that time, I wanted a secure 
job and a chance to use myeduca-
tion," he said. 
But his athletic exploits have not 
gone unnoticed. Councilman An-
derson's alma mater, Benedict Col-
lege in Columbia, South Carolina, 
inducted him into its Athletic Hall 
of Fame. Recalling his days as "the 
O.J. Simpson of my class," Ander-
son stated, "One of the most valua-
ble lessons I learned back then was 
to take the ball and not run over my 
opponent, but to run around him .. .I 
believe we should be students and 
athletes all of our lives." 
Councilman Anderson was ap-
pointed by the Carson City Council 
to fill the unexpired term of Coun-
cilman Tom Mills. Councilman Mills 
passed away last ApriL 


Carson News • June 1988 
Neighboorhood Watch Expo 
To Feature Protection Tips 
Personal, home and business pro-
tection will be the topic of a free 
Neighborhood Watch fair to be held 
June 18 from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m. at the 
Carson Community Center. 
Sponsored by the Sheriffs Sta-
tion and the City of Carson, the 
event will include displays and 
demonstrations on such topics as 
missing children, bicycle safety and 
licensing and safety for the elderly 
and disabled. Tips about building 
security, burglary prevention, false 
alarms and home and business 
security inspections will also be 
offered. 
The Sheriff sDepartment will have 
displays and demonstrations of 
some of its programs, such as the 
SWAT (Special Weapons Team) and 
K-9's (Police Dogs). Visitors will be 
able to get a first-hand look at some 
of the Sheriff and Fire Departments' 
equipment, including a chance to 
ride in the Fire Department's snor-
kellift. 
The "WeTip" display will have in-
formation on drugs, toxic crime, 
and crimes against children and 
Seniors. 
Free hot dogs, popcorn, soft 
drinks will be served and special 
prizes will be given away to promote 
the safety programs. 
Entertainment for the event will 
include the Carson Clowners, the 
Senior's band, the Young Life Sing-
ers, Los Caballeros Youth Band, 
Polynesian fire dancers and ventril-
oquist act, Williams & Co. 
The event will be held outside of 
the Community Center which is 
located at 801 E. Carson Street. For 
more information call the Commun-
ity Center at 835-0212. 
Local Runner Finishes 79th 
In L.A. Marathon 
Local Runner Martin Morino finished 79th out of more than 17,000 runners 
participating in the recent Los Angeles Marathon. Morino is a student at Cal-
State University, Dominguez HiJ1s and is a management trainee at J C. Penney·s. 
At a recent council meeting, Mr. Morino was honored for his outstanding 
achievement. Pictured from left to right are: Front row; M;wor K;W Calas, Martin 
Morino and M;wor Pro Tem Michael Mitoma. Back row, Councilwoman Sylvia 
Muise and Councilwoman Vera Robles DeWitt. 
Troubleshooter 
Each month this section of the 
newsletter will deal with a frequent-
ly asked question fielded by the staff 
at the City's Service Request Desk 
or will highlight important new in-
formation which might be of inter-
est to the general public. 
Question: 
What noises are considered "un-
necessary" under the City ordinance 
and after what hour is it unlawful to 
produce these noises? 
Answer: 
Unnecessary noises are sounds 
which are so harsh, prolonged or 
physically annoying that they cause 
discomfort to persons in the gen-
eral area where the noise is being 
made. Some noises that fall under 
these specifications are stereos, tele-
visions sets, band rehearsals, loud-
speakers and amplifiers, engines 
and motors, animals, construction 
devices or simply yelling and 
shouting. 
In general, these noises are pro-
hibited between the hours of 11 
p.m. and 7 a.m. But some, like an 
incessantly barking dog or person 
shouting obscene language, are 
always prohibited and may result in 
a citation to the person responsible. 
Some sounds, like that of advertis-
ing loudspeakers and construction 
machinery, must cease by 9 p.m. 


City Seeks 
A Few 
Good People 
APPLY NOW FOR SEATS 
ON CITY COMMISSIONS 
AND COMMITTEES 
The City of Carson is now seeking 
citizens interested in becoming in-
volved in local government as mem-
bers of various commissions and 
committees. 
Applications may be obtained at 
City Hall in the City Clerk's office. 
All applications will be retained 
for consideration as vacancies 
occur during the year. 
Service on commissions carries a 
$35 stipend, but those who sit on 
committees and boards do so on a 
volunteer basis. Carson residency 
and voter registration is required 
for membership on all commissions. 
The following groups are seeking 
members: 
More information may be obtain-
ed by contacting Helen Kawagoe at 
the City Clerk's office (830-7600). 
Carson News. June 1988 
Young Gymnast Chosen For 
USA Junior National Team 
At a recent meeting of the Carson City Council, gymnast Lily Garcia received a 
proclamation from the City in honor of her athletic achievement. Pictured from 
left to right are; Back row, Sylvia Muise, Councilwoman; John Anderson, 
Councilman and Vera Robles De Wi~ Councilwoman, Front row, Lily Garcia, 
gymnast and Michael Mitoma, Mayor Pro Tem. 
Lily Garcia, an outstanding 13-
year old gymnast from Carson, was 
recently chosen to compete on the 
USA Junior National Rhythmic 
Gymnastics Team. 
Gymnasts for the 8-girl team 
were chosen at a grueling 2-day 
competition in Marin County. Lily is 
California State and Regional Hoop 
Champion. This will be her first 
international competition. 
Lily trains for 4 hours a day; five 
days a week. Locally, she is on the 
West Coast Waves Team which is 
coached by J olie Barretta and Sandi 
Conley at Studio West in Palos 
Verdes. Also chosen from the Waves 
to compete with Lily, was Franca 
Abbatiello. Recently, Lily and Franca 
performed at the Compton Library. 
Other performances for Lily in· 
elude several cable T.V programs, 
and the USA/USSR Exhibition at 
UCLA. According to Lily, perform-
ing at UCLA was particularly excit-
ing because it gave her the oppor-
tunity to perform with top US and 
Soviet gymnasts, several of whom 
will be going to the Olympics. Lily, 
herself, is too young for the Olym-
pics this year-but who knows 
about 1992? 
Meanwhile Lily is concentrating 
on just having fun and getting the 
most out of gymnastics. "Rhythmic 
Gymnastics helps me feel creative 
and good about myself," she said. 
Her only goals for the moment are 
to have "high scores and clean rou· 
tines," which should help her when 
she competes in the Nationals. 


Fourth Of July 
Spectacular 
To Be Held 
At Dominguez 
Hills 
A Fourth of July show sponsored 
by the City of Carson will be held at 
5 p.m. in the Velodrome at Cal State 
Dominguez Hills. 
The program will start off with 
bicycle races and exhibitions by 
various youth and professional organ-
izations. The Carson-Dominguez 
Hills Symphony orchestra led by 
Dr. Frances Steiner will then per-
form from 8 p.m. through the dura-
tion of the fireworks show which 
begins at approximately 9 p.m. 
Free tickets and bus transporta-
tion will be provided by the City. For 
tickets and bus schedules contact 
City Hall or your neighborhood 
park. 
Remodeling 
Of City Hall 
To Be 
Completed in 
Mid-August 
Remodeling of Carson City Hall 
should be completed by the middle 
of August, according to Howard 
Homan, Director of Parks and 
Recreation. 
The improvements include addi-
tion of a new South Wing, painting 
inside and out, a new air-condition-
ing and electrical system, and an 
updated security system. 
The building's interior "W'ill have 
new wall covering, new carpet and 
new paint in both the council 
chambers and offices. 
Carson News. June 1988 
Resident Receives 
Medal Of Merit 
Pictured from left to right are Front Row: Mayor Kay Calas, Jason Carroll and 
Mayor Pro Tem Michael Mitoma. Back Row: Councilwoman Sylvia Muise and 
Councilwoman Vera Robles DeWitt. 
Only on rare occasions does the 
City of Carson award its highest 
tribute, The Medal of Merit. This 
Medal is often awarded to people 
who have endangered their own 
lives to help others. Recently, The 
Council presented this award to 
Jason Carroll. 
On March 15, Jason Carroll was 
visiting at the Paradise Trailer 
Lodge when a nearby mobile horne 
was engulfed in flames. Upon hear-
ing a child's scream through a win-
dow in the horne, Mr. Carroll smash-
ed the window in an attempt to 
rescue the child. The heat was too 
intense, however. Mr. Carroll then 
entered through a sliding door, 
where he discovered an elderly 
woman lying unconscious near the 
entryway. As he carried the uncon-
scious woman from the burning 
horne, the child jumped to safety 
through the window broken by Car-
roll, earlier. 
Through the heroic efforts of 
Jason Carroll, Mrs. Concepcion 
Bristol and her 3- year-old great 
grandson were saved from a certain 
death. 
Free Job Training, 
Placement Offered 
Free job training and placement 
is being offered by PIC works, a 
network of the Private Industry 
Councils of the cities of Carson, 
Lomita and Torrance. 
Membership to the program is 
free and the only eligibility require-
ments are that participants be laid 
off, unemployed or underemployed 
and economically disadvantaged. 
Residency in the sponsoring cities 
is also a requirement. 
Members will receive training in 
such areas as word processing, 
automotive repair and electronic 
assembly and may participate injob 
search workshops, skills testing and 
on-the-job training. 
For membership applications or 
more information visit PIC works at 
21818 S. Wilmingtion #408 or call 
830-3630. 


Carson News • June 1988 
City Of Carson Holds 
Annual Art Show 
Artist Edgar Buocree displays his technique of "pointillism" 
during a demonstration at the show. 
Residents attending the May 6 Artiest gaze intently at one of 
the many fine pieces of work displayed. 
Over 100 local artists were show-
cased in "Art Fest 88" held in the 
Carson Comm unity Center. A cham-
pagne reception started off the 
annual event which featured enter-
tainment by harpist Howard Rich's 
Saturday Night Bath, Johnny Blay-
lock's combo, singer Mary Nelson 
and harpist Lisel Erman. 
The artists displayed over 400 
pieces which were judged by faculty 
from Cal State Dominguez Hills, EI 
Camino and Harbor Colleges. The 
work, which included oil, acrylic, 
and watercolor painting; charcoal 
sketching; sculpture and ceramics 
was judged in 11 different cate-
gories with a total of 44 awards 
given to outstanding artists. 
Helen Bell, Assistant Coordinator 
of Senior Recreation at the Com-
munity Center, estimated total vis-
itor attendance at the event to be 
well over 450 people. Pieces were 
offered for sale or visitors, like stu-
dents in Carson elementary and 
junior high art classes, could just 
admire the work that the South Bay 
artists had to offer. 
"It was a great success," Bell said. 
"And we expect it to be even bigger 
next year." 
Ibis Hotel Brings 
"Taste Of France" To Carson 
"French Flair Comes To Carson" 
will be the theme of the grand open-
ing of the Ibis Hotel now under con-
struction in Carson. 
The grand opening is scheduled 
for the first two weeks in Septem-
ber, with entertainment and gala 
events to be announced. 
In additon to its 225 rooms, the 
Ibis will have 3,000 square feet of 
banquet space, with 200 person 
occupancy. The hotel will also fea-
ture two restaurants for dining and 
entertainment pleasure: Le Restau-
rante with authentic French cui-
sine and Claudine's Lounge with 
live music and dancing. 
The hotel will be close to the new 
Carson Community Center which 
will accommodate larger parties for 
banquets and conventions. For 
more information about the open-
ing or the hotel, itself, call the Ibis at 
(213) 830-9200.

Trouble 
Shooter 
Each month this section of the news-
letter will deal with a frequently asked 
question fielded by the staff at the 
City's Service Request Desk, or will 
highlight important new information 
which might be of interest to the' 
general public. 
QUESTION: 
How many and what breeds of animals 
are city residents able to keep at their 
homes? 
ANSWER: 
The following list includes which 
type of animals and how many of each 
are allowed in a family residence. 
• Cats 
• Dogs 
• Tropical Fish 
• No more than 20 white mice 
• No more than any three of the 
following: 
Canaries 
Chinchillas 
Chipmunks 
Finches 
Gopher 
Snakes 
Ginney Pigs 
Hamsters 
Hawks 
King Snakes 
Marmo Monkeys 
Pigeons 
Rabbits 
Ravens 
Monkeys 
Squirrel Monkeys 
Toucans 
Turtles 
White Doves 
The service request desk is a clear-
ing house for any questions residents 
may have regarding the City of Car-
son. If you are in need of a particular 
service the City has to offer or would 
like more information regarding this 
issue please call 830-7600, ext. 320. 
The Carson Report -January 1988 
Carson 
Job-Hunters 
Get Lucky 
Are you ready to return to the work-
force but find that you don't have the 
technical skills? Do you just need to 
brush up on your typing? Or maybe 
you have the skills but just can't find 
the right opening? 
The Job Training Center, located at 
21818 Wilmington in Carson, may be 
the answer to your problems. 
This special program is jointly spon-
sored by the cities of Carson, Torrance 
and Lomita, and has been in existance 
since 1983. Carson's fourth year of par-
ticipation in the federal program, 
which is called the Private Industry 
Council, was again recognized as one 
of the most effective programs in the 
nation. The Private Industry Council's 
efforts in the program results in the 
placement of youths in summer jobs 
with local firms and has registered an 
estimated 800 youths for the program. 
The Job Training Center's purpose is 
to teach new job skills to low-income 
people, dislocated workers, displaced 
homemakers and those with employ-
ment barriers. The Center offers 
vocational training in the classroom in 
many different areas, such as elec-
tronics and computer skills. They then 
assist participants in finding jobs that 
match their new job skills. By working 
with area companies, administrators 
are often able to place their graduates 
in well-paid jobs. 
The Job Training Center points with 
pride to the fact that participants have 
a 70% employment rate after some 
training, at an average wage of $8.09. 
Enrollment in this program is lim-
ited. Interested residents should call 
830-3630. 
City Adopts 1987-88 Fiscal Budget 
The Carson City Council, after 
numerous public hearings and work-
shops, has adopted the City's budget 
for fiscal year 1987-88. 
Funds come from 
of 
sources, including revenues from local 
taxes, state and federal funds, and 
"users fees" such as business licenses. 
Carson will receive additional assis-
tance under new legislation passed by 
the State Legislature which allocates 
money to no- and low-tax cities such as 
Carson, which shares its revenue base 
with the County of Los Angeles. 
The total of all of Carson's budgets is 
$29,368,337, which includes the Gen-
eral 
Fund 
Operating 
Budget of 
Other budgets total 
$4,004,211. 
Carson Cleans 
Up City With 
Graffiti 
Abatement 
Program 
Last summer, the Carson City 
Council began a new program to 
remove graffiti from City-owned and 
private property. Known as the Graffiti 
Abatement Program, this innovative 
idea allows City maintenance workers 
and private citizens to work together 
to clean up Carson. 
The City's Graffiti Abatement Crew 
is responsible for removing graffiti 
from walls, fences, and buildings 
which are adjacent to or facing a public 
sidewalk or street. The City will also 
provide, at no charge to residents, a 
gallon of paint and a roller or brush so 
that residents may remove graffiti from 
their own property. 
The City Crew works in seven 
graffiti maintenance zones throughout 
Carson in a sequential order, and com-
pletes work in one zone before moving 
on to the next zone. You may contact 
Pete Mendoza at 830-7600, extension 
385, to find out when the crew will be 
in your area, or for more information 
on the Graffiti Abatement Program. 
The Carson 20th 
Anniversary 
Committee Is 
Looking For 
Historic Photos 
As part of the City's 20th Anniver-
sary activities, the Anniversary Com-
mittee is planning to assemble a photo 
history of the City's last 20 years. A 
photo display of people and events 
important to Carson's history will be 
unveiled at the Anniversary Ball, Feb-
ruary 27, 1988. 
If you have photos of events pertain-
ing to the last 20 years of the 
history and would like to lend them to 
the Anniversary Committee as part of 
the 
contact the Public 
Information 
Office 
at 
830-7600, 
ext. 315.

sections.question:
You asked the following questions about the January and 
June 1988 copies of the City of Carson monthly report entitled 
liThe Carson Report. II 
1. 
May the city publish portrait photographs of its 
elected officials in the monthly report? 
2. 
May the city publish group photographs of the city's 
elected officials in the report, if the officials are not 
identified by name? 
3. 
May the city publish a feature article introducing a 
newly appointed councilmember? 
Y, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code 
of Regulations. 
 • (916)322-5660 


William R. McKown 
August 19, 1988 
Page -2-
4. 
Is there a difference between a report being mailed at 
public expense and being delivered at public expense?
sections.conclusion:
The city may not publish photographs of or otherwise refer 
to elected officers in the city's monthly report or in any 
other mass mailing. 
This prohibition includes publishing group 
photographs in which elected city officers are not identified 
by name. 
Furthermore, for purposes of the Act a person 
appointed to a vacant elective office is an elected officer. 
Therefore, the city may not publish a feature article about a 
newly appointed councilmember. 
Nevertheless, the city may list the names of elected 
officers in a standard roster-type listing, as long as names of 
all elected officers are in the same type size and typeface. 
The Commission also has concluded that a public agency may 
not send a mass mailing in any way that results in expenditure 
of public moneys. 
Delivering a document is a form of sending a 
document. 
Therefore, the city may not spend public moneys to 
deliver mass mailings that contain the name, signature, or 
photograph of an elected officer.
sections.facts:
The city of Carson publishes a monthly report entitled "The 
Carson Report." 
On the address page of the January 1988 
report, the city published captioned portrait photographs of 
the city's seven elected officers: 
five councilmembers, the 
city clerk and city treasurer. 
The June 1988 report had several references to elected city 
officers. 
That issue's front-page feature article introduced a 
newly appointed member of the city council; the article 
included a large portrait photograph of the official. 
Inside 
the report, three different group photographs showed several 
councilmembers, who were identified by name in the captions. 
Finally, the address page had captioned portrait photographs of 
the city's seven elected officers.
sections.analysis:
California electors enacted proposition 73 in June 1988. 
Proposition 73 includes amendments to Sections 89001 and 


William R. McKown 
August 19, 1988 
Page -3-
82041.5 of the Act. 
These amendments took effect on June 8, 
1988. 
section 89001 now reads: 
89001. 
No newsletter or other mass mailing shall 
be sent at public expense. 
section 82041.5 now reads: 
82041.5. 
"Mass mailing" means two hundred or 
more substantially similar pieces of mail, but does 
not include a form letter or other mail which is sent 
in response to an unsolicited request, letter or other 
inquiry. 
These two sections could be construed to ban all 
mailings at public expense of 200 or more substantially 
similar documents. 
On July 26, 1988, however, the 
Commission adopted an emergency regulation and approved 
interim advice that allows public agencies to send certain 
mass mailings at public expense. 
(Regulation 18901; Raye 
Advice Letters, No. A-88-220, copies enclosed.) 
In the 
following paragraphs we apply emergency Regulation 18901 
and the interim advice letters to the monthly reports 
published by the city of Carson. 
Prohibition Against Spending Public Moneys To Promote Elected 
Officials 
After considering ballot arguments in favor of Proposition 
73, statements by the proponents of the proposition, and the 
language of amended section 89001, the Commission concluded 
that the intent of section 89001 is to prohibit the use of 
public moneys for the purpose of sending out mass mailings 
which assist elected officials in the quest for public office. 
(Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, June 16, 1988.). 
Therefore, 
any reference to an elected officer is prohibited in a public 
agency newsletter sent to 200 or more persons in a calendar 
month, except in the standard letterhead or in a roster-type 
listing in which each elected officer's name appears in the 
same type size and typeface as the other officers. 
(See 
emergency Regulation 18901; Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, 
July 1, 1988.) 
Consequently, the city may not publish portrait or group 
photographs of elected officers in the monthly report, even if 
the captions do not identify the officers by name. 
Also, 
Section 82020 defines an elected officer to include "a person 
who is appointed to fill a vacant elective office .... " 
Thus, a 


William R. McKown 
August 19, 1988 
Page -4-
newly appointed councilmember is an elected officer for 
purposes of the Act. 
Therefore, the city cannot publish and 
send a feature article about the newly appointed councilmember 
or use a photograph of the officer. 
(Also see Gonzalves Advice 
Letter, No. A-88-021, copy enclosed.) 
The city however, may 
list its elected officers in the standard letterhead or in a 
roster-type listing that uses the same type size and typeface 
for each officer. 
Sending A Mass Mailing 
For the meantime, the Commission has concluded that section 
89001 prohibits a public agency from spending public moneys to 
distribute mass mailings that contain the names or photographs 
of elected officers. 
(Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, July 
1, 1988, at page 5.) 
The terms "sent" and "mailing" include 
delivering documents. (Raye Advice Letter, No. A-88-220, July 
I, 1988, at page 6, footnote 4.) 
Therefore, the city may not 
use public moneys to pay for any type of delivery service to 
distribute a mass mailing containing the name or photograph of 
an elected officer. 
I hope this letter provides satisfactory answers to your 
advice request. 
Please call me at (916) 322-5901 if you have 
any questions about this letter. 
DMG:MA:aa 
Enclosures

QUESTION: You asked the following questions about the January and 
June 1988 copies of the City of Carson monthly report entitled 
liThe Carson Report. II 
1. 
May the city publish portrait photographs of its 
elected officials in the monthly report? 
2. 
May the city publish group photographs of the city's 
elected officials in the report, if the officials are not 
identified by name? 
3. 
May the city publish a feature article introducing a 
newly appointed councilmember? 
Y, et seq. 
All references to 
regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code 
of Regulations. 
 • (916)322-5660 


William R. McKown 
August 19, 1988 
Page -2-
4. 
Is there a difference between a report being mailed at 
public expense and being delivered at public expense?

CONCLUSION: The city may not publish photographs of or otherwise refer 
to elected officers in the city's monthly report or in any 
other mass mailing. 
This prohibition includes publishing group 
photographs in which elected city officers are not identified 
by name. 
Furthermore, for purposes of the Act a person 
appointed to a vacant elective office is an elected officer. 
Therefore, the city may not publish a feature article about a 
newly appointed councilmember. 
Nevertheless, the city may list the names of elected 
officers in a standard roster-type listing, as long as names of 
all elected officers are in the same type size and typeface. 
The Commission also has concluded that a public agency may 
not send a mass mailing in any way that results in expenditure 
of public moneys. 
Delivering a document is a form of sending a 
document. 
Therefore, the city may not spend public moneys to 
deliver mass mailings that contain the name, signature, or 
photograph of an elected officer.
embedding.first_500_words: California Fair Political Practices Commission William R. McKown Acting city Administrator City of Carson P.O. Box 6234 Carson, CA 90749 Dear Mr. McKown: August 19, 1988 Re: Your Request For Advice Our File No. A-88-276 You have requested advice about application of section 89001 of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")Y, as amended by recently enacted Proposition 73, to the city of Carson monthly report. QUESTIONS You asked the following questions about the January and June 1988 copies of the City of Carson monthly report entitled liThe Carson Report. II 1. May the city publish portrait photographs of its elected officials in the monthly report? 2. May the city publish group photographs of the city's elected officials in the report, if the officials are not identified by name? 3. May the city publish a feature article introducing a newly appointed councilmember? Y Government Code sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 William R. McKown August 19, 1988 Page -2- 4. Is there a difference between a report being mailed at public expense and being delivered at public expense? CONCLUSIONS The city may not publish photographs of or otherwise refer to elected officers in the city's monthly report or in any other mass mailing. This prohibition includes publishing group photographs in which elected city officers are not identified by name. Furthermore, for purposes of the Act a person appointed to a vacant elective office is an elected officer. Therefore, the city may not publish a feature article about a newly appointed councilmember. Nevertheless, the city may list the names of elected officers in a standard roster-type listing, as long as names of all elected officers are in the same type size and typeface. The Commission also has concluded that a public agency may not send a mass mailing in any way that results in expenditure of public moneys. Delivering a document is a form of sending a document. Therefore, the city may not spend public moneys to deliver mass mailings that contain the name, signature, or photograph of an elected officer. FACTS The city of Carson publishes a monthly report entitled "The Carson Report." On the address page of the January 1988 report, the city published captioned portrait photographs of the city's seven elected officers: five councilmembers, the city clerk and city treasurer. The June 1988 report had several references to elected city officers. That issue's front-page feature article introduced a newly appointed member of the city council; the article included a large portrait photograph of the official. Inside the report, three different group photographs showed several councilmembers, who were identified by name in the captions. Finally, the address page had captioned portrait photographs of the city's seven elected officers. ANALYSIS
